layers.content: Un mes adentro de la selva más grande del mundo.
El río puede subir hasta 12 metros e inundar la selva por meses enteros.
La selva tropical más grande del mundo.
5,5 millones de kilómetros cuadrados de vida.
Cuando llega la lluvia, todo el pueblo sale a recibirla.
La lluvia aquí no interrumpe la vida. Es la vida.
No hay carreteras. Solo el río.
El Amazonas es la única vía.
Un partido. Una fiesta. Su unión.
Viajan en grupos de hasta 75 individuos por el dosel amazónico.
Su piel contiene suficiente toxina para paralizar a un predador.
El Amazonas colombiano alberga más de 600 especies de aves.
Vulnerable. Dispersa semillas esenciales para la regeneración del bosque.
Un encuentro inesperado: un grupo, río arriba.
Torturados por décadas para la investigación del Dr. Patarroyo. Hoy, en Vista Alegre, libres.
Uno de los pájaros más vistosos y menos conocidos del Amazonas.
Un macho en plena danza de cortejo. Un momento que pocos logran presenciar.
Sin bolsa marsupial. Sus crías se aferran directamente a las mamas.

image_index.description: Sheet 5, image 5. Series opener. Walkway suspended in the canopy; the structure and treetops dominate the upper two-thirds. Negative space: dark lower third where the forest floor falls into shadow. Gradient to-bottom from 40% creates contrast for the headline. Focal point x:50 y:35 keeps the walkway and canopy visible while the lower zone stays open for text. Headline names the place; sub gives personal context.

Sheet 3, image 2. Environmental frame establishing forest scale. Dense trunks fill the frame; the subject is the tree mass itself. Negative space: diffuse sky and canopy glow at the top. Gradient to-top from 60% darkens only the upper portion where text lives. Focal point x:50 y:40. Eyebrow names the place and forest type; body delivers the flood-height fact that the image alone cannot communicate.

Sheet 5, image 6. Aerial overview establishing the river system at scale. River curve and immediate forest dominate the lower portion; a band of sky sits above the horizon. Gradient to-top from 50% darkens the sky band for the headline. Focal point x:50 y:55 keeps the river bend centered. Headline announces the system globally; body gives the one figure that contextualizes the aerial view.

Sheet 4, image 8. Silent pause frame between the forest and people sections. Canoe silhouette in the lower third against a golden sky. No text, no overlay — the image's own contrast resolves the composition. Focal point x:50 y:65 keeps the canoe in frame and maximizes the golden sky above.

Sheet 1, image 3. First human frame. Child's face centered in the upper area; lower third falls into dark negative space. Gradient to-bottom from 40%. Focal point x:45 y:40 keeps the face in the upper-center area. Three text layers justified by the gap between what the camera captured (one quiet child) and what actually happened (the whole village celebrating): eyebrow gives location, body narrates the event, sub gives the meaning.

Sheet 5, image 8. River-transport frame. Vessel centered in frame; sky above is the negative space. Gradient to-top from 55%. Focal point x:50 y:50 centers the vessel. Headline delivers the conceptual punchline about the absence of roads; sub reinforces with the single-clause fact.

Sheet 1, image 10. Community action frame. Players fill the lower portion; sky above is the quiet zone. Gradient to-top from 60% darkens sky only. Focal point x:50 y:55 keeps the action in frame. Eyebrow anchors to Mocagua; body loads meaning onto the action before the eye reaches the players — sport becomes celebration becomes community in three beats.

Sheet 2, image 9. Emotional breathing room. Faces fill the frame with no clean negative space that does not compete with the expression. No overlay, no text — the laughter is the entire message. Focal point x:50 y:40 centers the faces. Intentional silence after two community frames with text.

Sheet 1, image 6. Fauna section opener. Monkey in the upper area; lower third is the quiet zone. Gradient to-bottom from 50%. Focal point x:50 y:40. Scientific name in amber italic identifies the species; dato gives the social behavior number that contextualizes what the image shows.

Sheet 2, image 6. Macro fauna frame. Frog and leaf fill the frame; the lower edge is darkened by a strong gradient (to_opacity 0.70) needed because the leaf surface is highly reflective. Focal point x:50 y:50 centers the frog. Scientific name identifies to genus; dato delivers the toxicity fact without hyperbole.

Sheet 2, image 3. Bird frame with compositional tension. Bird positioned on the right side of frame (focal point x:65 y:50); the lower-left corner is the quiet zone for text. Gradient to-bottom from 50%. The bird's gaze directs toward the right while text anchors left — intentional directional tension. Scientific name corrects the common misidentification as a kingfisher.

Sheet 1, image 4. Primate close-up near Fundación Maikuchiga, Mocagua. Face and eyes dominate the upper area; lower third is the quiet zone. Gradient to-bottom from 55%. Focal point x:50 y:45. Three text layers: eyebrow gives location and conservation context, scientific name identifies the species, dato states the IUCN status and ecological role. Mocagua appears second time — the village that plays soccer also lives alongside one of the most threatened primates in the Amazon.

Sheet 1, image 8. The only frame shot outside Colombia, on the Peruvian bank at Vista Alegre. Dolphin in the lower half of frame; water and sky above are the quiet zone. Gradient to-top from 50%. Focal point x:50 y:55 keeps the dolphin in frame. Three layers: eyebrow marks the border crossing, scientific name, dato captures the spontaneity. The Amazon does not stop at the Colombian border.

Sheet 1, image 9. Group of three Aotus vociferans (not Cebuella pygmaea) at Vista Alegre, Perú. Group centered in frame; lower edge is the quiet zone. Gradient to-bottom from 55%. Focal point x:50 y:45. Three text layers: eyebrow continues the Peruvian location thread from frame-13, scientific name corrects the common misidentification, dato tells the full story of their capture and recovery under Dr. Patarroyo's research program.

Sheet 3, image 4. Bird facing right at Lago Tarapoto. Subject centered, looking right; lower-left corner is the quiet zone for text. Gradient to-bottom from 50%. Focal point x:50 y:45 keeps the crimson breast visible. The bird's rightward gaze creates tension with the left-anchored text block — eye exits right, text pulls it back left. Puerto Nariño appears for the third time.

Sheet 3, image 5. Male in courtship display at Lago el Correo. Bird showing red dorsal plumage and wire filaments; lower zone is quiet. Gradient to-bottom from 50%. Focal point x:50 y:50. Three text layers: eyebrow anchors to location, scientific name, dato captures the rarity of witnessing the display. Puerto Nariño appears fourth time: trogon (stillness) then manakin (motion) — contrasting registers, same place.

Sheet 5, image 2. Frog on a leaf, centered. No usable negative space that does not compete with the subject. No overlay, no text — intentional silence after two consecutive bird frames with text. The image is complete on its own. Focal point x:50 y:45. The viewer breathes before the series moves to dusk and night.

Sheet 3, image 10. Heron in golden-hour light — the transition frame between day and night. Flat overlay at 0.10 opacity only (no gradient) preserves the warm amber tones. Focal point x:50 y:50. A single scientific name in amber italic is the only text: the amber color of the type echoes the golden light of the image. Deliberate restraint after eleven fauna frames with data.

Sheet 2, image 7. Nocturnal frame — the arrival of night. Face illuminated against an absolute black background. Two flat overlays (#0D1F14 at 0.20 + #0A0510 at 0.15) unify the surrounding darkness without adding a directional gradient. Focal point x:50 y:40 centers the illuminated face. Scientific name identifies the species; dato delivers the marsupial fact that subverts the viewer's assumption.

Sheet 5, image 7. Series closer. Woman holding an anaconda — an unscripted moment. Subject centered; lower third is the quiet zone. Gradient to-bottom from 40%. Focal point x:50 y:45 keeps both woman and anaconda in frame. Two text layers (confirmed): headline delivers the verdict on the place in one compressed thought; location tag grounds it geographically. Echoes frame-01: opens with a name, closes with a truth.
